title: practical english usage - Part 157
content:
And as much  ... can be used as an adverb.

/ ate as much  as I could. She didn't catch as many as she'd hoped.

As much/many can be used without following nouns.

We need as many people as possible.

I haven't got as much  money as I thought.

We can use as much/many... as to talk about quantity.

5 as  much/many           ...  as modern English.

A  subject form without a verb (e.g. as well as he) is unusual in this structure in She doesn't sing as well as I do.

In a formal style, we  prefer subject + verb after as.

She doesn't sing as well as me.

In an informal style we  can use object pronouns (me, him,  etc) after as.

4 pronouns       after    as We'll do as much  as practicable before the end of the week.

I'll spend as much  as necessary. You're as beautiful as ever.

Please get here as soon as possible.

using words with similar meanings).

The  structures as... as possible/necessarylever/needed are common (and others 3 as  ... as  possible,         etc He's not as/so friendly as she is. (more informal than He's less friendly...) After not, we  can use so ... as instead of as . .. as.

2 negative       structures Take as much  time as you need.

She speaks Spanish as well as the rest of us.

She's as tall as her brother. Is it as good as you expected?

We   use as. .. as to say that people or things are equal in some way.

203 as...    as;  as   much     /many      as             ____ grammar •  203   as... as; as much/many as For sentences like (As) cold as it was, we went out, ►    255.

For as replacing subject or object (e.g. as many people as want it), ►    256.

For the word order in sentences like She's as good a dancer as her brother, ►     187.

For as long as, ►    379.       For as well as, ►    382.

Note that as is usually pronounced /əz/   (►   315).

She's hard as nails.

The  first as can be dropped in these expressions in an informal style.

as cold as ice as black as night as hard as nails We   use the structure as... as... in a lot of traditional comparative expressions.

11   traditional        expressions:        as  cold     as   ice In cases like this, not so is much more common than not as.

I used to think he was clever. Now I'm not so sure.

The train takes 40 minutes. By car it'll take you twice as long.

meaning is clear from what comes before.

The  second part of the as ... as or so ... as structure can be left out when the 10 leaving      out   the   second      part:   twice      as   long     ...

If you married me, I'd give you as much  freedom as you wanted.

We'll get there as soon as you do/will.

infinitive (►  231).

to refer to the future, and a past tense can have a meaning similar to would + In as... ɑs-elauses (and other kinds of ɑs-elauses), a present tense is often used 9 tenses I'm not quite as tired as I was last week.

She's every bit as beautiful as her sister.

You're nothing like as bad-tempered as you used to be.

It's not nearly as cold as yesterday. He's just as strong as ever.

exactly, not quite.

Before as... as we  can use (not) nearly, almost, just, nothing like, every bit, 8  modification:        nearly       as   ...

expected,  ►    208.3) or It took three times as long as I expected, (   ... three times longer than I I'm not going out with a man who's twice as old as me.

You're not half as clever as you think you are.

Half, twice, three times, etc can be used before as... as.

big    bigger    biggest One vowel + one consonant: fat   fatter   fattest nice   nicer    nicest      J  + -r, -st.
